figures.caption: Fig. 3. For k = 2, we visualize the raw trajectories ϕt over time. LRM-Functao exhibits spiral-like trajectories, whereas LRM-Functab collapses the trajectory to a line. Projecting ϕt onto the principal motion direction p and filtering yields the 1D signal sfilt, which enables direct interpretable identification of ED and ES frames.

Fig. 1. Overview of our LRM-Functa framework. A) Our approach builds on VidFuncta, where each ultrasound video is represented by a video-level modulation vector v and time-resolved modulation vectors ϕ = {ϕt}Tt=1. (B) Pairwise cosine similarity of ϕ over time reveals a clear low-rank pattern. (C) Motivated by this observation, we constrain the latent modulation space to a low-rank subspace Bβ . (D/E) This structured latent space enables efficient compression-reconstruction and cardiac phase analysis.
Table 1. Mean ± standard deviation for the ED and ES MAE on the EchoNet-Dynamic and the OOD POCUS test sets. All methods are unsupervised.
Table 2. Mean ± std of downstream performance on V̂, evaluated on the reconstructed EchoNet-Dynamic and LUS test sets using 5-fold cross-validation.

Fig. 2. Proposed architecture for low-rank adaptation of the modulation vectors. Each video V is encoded into a video-level representation v and time-resolved frame representations ϕt, which are projected onto a rank-k subspace Bβ to produce modulation vectors mt = v+Bβϕt. These vectors shift the activations of a shared MLP Mθ, trained to reconstruct pixel intensities ẑ at each coordinate (x, y), shown as a red cross.
